perturbation.original_text: SHPS shall provide the Care Management Services directly to the Clients in accordance with the terms of the Client Agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: SHPS shall endeavor to provide the Care Management Services to the Clients, taking into account the terms of the Client Agreements.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall provide' to 'shall endeavor to provide' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. Adding 'taking into account' further reduces the certainty of compliance with the Client Agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 456.011(1)(a) requires healthcare providers to adhere to specific standards of care. By changing the language to 'endeavor to provide' and 'taking into account,' SHPS is no longer strictly obligated to meet those standards defined within client agreements, potentially jeopardizing patient care and violating the statute's intent for ensuring quality healthcare services.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXII > Chapter 456 > Section 011 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXII REGULATION OF PROFESSIONS AND OCCUPATIONS Chapter 456 HEALTH PROFESSIONS AND OCCUPATIONS: GENERAL PROVISIONS Entire Chapter SECTION 011 Boards; organization; meetings; compensation and travel expenses. F.S. 456.011 456.011 Boards; organization; meetings; compensation and travel expenses. — (1) Each board within the department shall comply with the provisions of this chapter. (2) The board shall annually elect from among its number a chairperson and vice chairperson. (3) The board shall meet at least once annually and may meet as often as is necessary. Meetings shall be conducted through teleconferencing or other technological means, unless disciplinary hearings involving standard of care, sexual misconduct, fraud, impairment, or felony convictions; licensure denial hearings; or controversial rule hearings are being conducted; or unless otherwise approved in advance of the meeting by the director of the Division of Medical Quality Assurance. The chairperson or a quorum of the board shall have the authority to call meetings, except as provided above relating to in-person meetings. A quorum shall be necessary for the conduct of official business by the board or any committee thereof. Unless otherwise provided by law, 51 percent or more of the appointed members of the board or any committee, when applicable, shall constitute a quorum. The membership of committees of the board, except as otherwise authorized pursuant to this chapter or the applicable practice act, shall be composed of currently appointed members of the board. The vote of a majority of the members of the quorum shall be necessary for any official action by the board or committee. Three consecutive unexcused absences or absences constituting 50 percent or more of the board’s meetings within any 12-month period shall cause the board membership of the member in question to become void, and the position shall be considered vacant. The board, or the department when there is no board, shall, by rule, define unexcused absences. (4) Unless otherwise provided by law, a board member or former board member serving on a probable cause panel shall be compensated $50 for each day in attendance at an official meeting of the board
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates that SHPS 'shall provide' Care Management Services, creating a clear obligation. The changed language weakens this obligation to 'endeavor to provide,' introducing ambiguity. This ambiguity directly contradicts the intent of Florida Statutes § 456.011(1)(a), as explained by the LLM, which requires healthcare providers to adhere to specific standards of care. While the scraped law snippet (Chapter 456 Section 011 - 2011 Florida Statutes) focuses on board organization and meetings, the LLM explanation connects the weakening of the obligation to potential jeopardization of patient care, implying a failure to meet required healthcare standards. The change from 'shall' to 'endeavor' creates a loophole that undermines the enforceability of the client agreements and potentially puts SHPS in conflict with healthcare standards, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: HPS shall preserve adequate records of Care Management Revenues by Client.
perturbation.changed_text: HPS should maintain records of Care Management Revenues by Client to a reasonable extent.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall preserve adequate records' with 'should maintain records...to a reasonable extent' makes the obligation discretionary and introduces ambiguity. 'Reasonable extent' is vague and unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 215.97 mandates the retention of financial records for state agencies and contractors. Changing 'shall preserve adequate records' to 'should maintain records to a reasonable extent' introduces ambiguity and discretion, undermining the statute's requirement for thorough and reliable financial documentation for proper auditing and oversight.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'shall preserve adequate records' with 'should maintain records...to a reasonable extent.' This introduces ambiguity and discretion where the original text mandated a clear obligation. The Law Explanation states that Florida Statutes § 215.97 requires thorough and reliable financial documentation. By making the record-keeping discretionary and vague, the changed text undermines the statute's requirement for proper auditing and oversight, as the term 'reasonable extent' lacks a clear, enforceable definition. Although a scraped law snippet is not provided, the Law Explanation sufficiently describes the legal contradiction based on the information provided.

perturbation.original_text: Where agreement, approval, acceptance, consent, or similar action by either party is required by any provision of this Agreement, such action shall not be unreasonably delayed or withheld.
perturbation.changed_text: Where agreement, approval, acceptance, consent, or similar action by either party is required by any provision of this Agreement, such action should not be delayed or withheld without due consideration.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not be unreasonably delayed or withheld' to 'should not be delayed or withheld without due consideration' introduces ambiguity. The term 'due consideration' is vague and subjective, allowing a party to delay or withhold agreement based on their own interpretation of what constitutes 'due consideration'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 671.203 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance or enforcement of contracts. This imposes some implied standard of 'reasonableness' in contractual dealings. By softening the language to 'should not be delayed...without due consideration,' allows for more subjective interpretation. This creates an easy means to not act in good faith by claiming some form of 'due consideration' was not met.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 6-103. APPLICABILITY OF ARTICLE. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 6-103. APPLICABILITY OF ARTICLE. (1) Except as otherwise provided in subsection (3), this Article applies to a bulk sale if: (a) the seller's principal business is the sale of inventory from stock; and (b) on the date of the bulk-sale agreement the seller is located in this state or, if the seller is located in a jurisdiction that is not a part of the United States , the seller's major executive office in the United States is in this state. (2) A seller is deemed to be located at his [or her] place of business. If a seller has more than one place of business, the seller is deemed located at his [or her] chief executive office. (3) This Article does not apply to: (a) a transfer made to secure payment or performance of an obligation; (b) a transfer of collateral to a secured party pursuant to Section 9-609 ; (c) a sale of collateral pursuant to Section 9-610 ; (d) retention of collateral pursuant to Section 9-620 ; (e) a sale of an asset encumbered by a security interest or lien if (i) all the proceeds of the sale are applied in partial or total satisfaction of the debt secured by the security interest or lien or (ii) the security interest or lien is enforceable against the asset after it has been sold to the buyer and the net contract price is zero; (f) a general assignment for the benefit of creditors or to a subsequent transfer by the assignee; (g) a sale by an executor, administrator, receiver, trustee in bankruptcy, or any public officer under judicial process; (h) a sale made in the course of judicial or administrative proceedings for the dissolution or reorganization of an organization; (i) a sale to a buyer whose principal place of business is in the United States and who: (i) not earlier than 21 days before the date of the bulk sale , (A) obtains from the seller a verified and dated list of claimants of whom the seller has notice three days before the seller sends or delivers the list to the buyer or (B) conducts a reasonable inquiry to discover the claimants; (ii) assumes in full the debts owed to claimants of whom the buyer has knowledge on the date the buyer receives the list of claimants from the seller or on the date the buyer completes the reasonable inquiry, as the case may be; (iii) is not insolvent after the assumption; and (iv) gives written notice of the assumption not later than 30 days after the date of the bulk sale by sending or delivering a notice to the claimants identified in subparagraph (ii) or by filing a notice in the office of the [Secretary of State]; (j) a sale to a buyer whose principal place of business is in the United States and who: (i) assumes in full the debts that were incurred in the seller's business before the date of the bulk sale ; (ii) is not insolvent after the assumption; and (iii) gives written notice of the assumption not later than 30 days after the date of the bulk sale by sending or delivering a notice to each creditor whose debt is assumed or by filing a notice in the office of the [Secretary of State]; (k) a sale to a new organization that is organized to take over and continue the business of the seller and that has its principal place of business in the United States if: (i) the buyer assumes in full the debts that were incurred in the seller's business before the date of the bulk sale ; (ii) the seller receives nothing from the sale except an interest in the new organization that is subordinate to the claims against the organization arising from the assumption; and (iii) the buyer gives written notice of the assumption not later than 30 days after the date of the bulk sale by sending or delivering a notice to each creditor whose debt is assumed or by filing a notice in the office of the [Secretary of State]; (l) a sale of assets having: (i) a value , net of liens and security interests, of less than $10,000. perturbation.justification: The change from "shall not be unreasonably delayed or withheld" to "should not be delayed or withheld without due consideration" weakens the obligation, creating ambiguity and potentially allowing for delays based on subjective interpretations. The LLM law explanation points to Florida Statutes § 671.203, which imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance or enforcement of contracts. The modified language, by introducing 'due consideration,' provides a loophole that could be exploited to avoid acting in good faith, thus contradicting the implied standard of reasonableness required by law. The altered text facilitates potential non-compliance with the good faith obligation because the 'due consideration' standard lacks a clear, objective benchmark, making it easier to justify delays or withholding actions without violating the literal terms of the contract.
